person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
John-1 Bidwell: End-of-Line Ancestor of Uncertain Origins

The origins of John Bidwell of Hartford, Connecticut are uncertain (Robert Charles Anderson, FASG [Fellow of the American Society of Genealogists], The Great Migration Directory: Immigrants to New England, 1620–1640; a Concise Compendium [Boston: New England Historical and Genealogical Society, 2015], 29).

For more information, please refer to the chapter entitled, “John Bidwell [of] Hartford, Connecticut,” within Donald Lines Jacobus [Fellow of the American Society of Genealogists] and Edgar Francis Waterman, Hale House and Related Families (Hartford: The Connecticut Historical Society, 1952), online at HathiTrust.org, beginning at page 459:

https://hdl.handle.net/2027/wu.89066151523?urlappend=%3Bseq=481

See also:

"John Bidwell, Hartford Founder"
Compiled by Timothy Lester Jacobs, SDFH Genealogist

https://www.foundersofhartford.org/the-founders/john-bidwell/


=== Unverified Content ===

Among the list of the earliest settlers of Windsor (http://dunhamwilcox.net/ct/puritan2.htm) there are the following:
John Biddolph (Buddulph was an earlier version of the family name)
John Bissell (juror in 1640-43—came to Windsor in 1639—deputy and ferryman, in '40.)
John Biddall (1639—landholder)
These three are almost certainly the same person.
Also on the list is a James Bissell.

An early map of Windsor (1640-1654) show John Bissell Sr holding a plot of land north on the main Windsor road and almost at Gunn's Brook. Also near what was the first ferry in the town.
See: https://www.werelate.org/images/e/ef/Map_Windsor-1654.jpg
also:
https://www.google.com/search?q=map+of+Windsor,+Connecticut+1640-1654&client=firefox-b-d&sxsrf=ACYBGNQ2YsgvP42I0wzTDRXe3gDfcfWiWA:1577811296574&tbm=isch&source=iu&ictx=1&fir=cyJyNQWjWy5emM%253A%252CvsiY5jJK6An9bM%252C_&vet=1&usg=AI4_-kQmhApcttEcjh0cVIWN6VksCtGy8w&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjK_-qOreDmAhWSWM0KHXAlBmkQ9QEwAnoECAkQCQ#imgrc=zTPWUiYkbvFStM:&vet=1

John Bidwell was a proprietor of Hartford and his name is on the Founders Monument. He received a grant of land "by the courtesie of the town" in 1640 on the east side of the road to the mill and a "tan-yard" on an island in the Little River, totaling four acres in all. He was a chimney-viewer in 1655 & 1661 and freed from training in 1670. [Chimney-viewers clean chimneys.]
He married Sarah Wilcock who died June 15, 1690, daughter of John & Mary Wilcock. He was an early member of the Center Church, but they removed & became members of the Second Church on Feb. 12, 1670.

Their children:
1. John m. Sarah Welles
2. Joseph m. Mary Colefax
3. Samuel d. 1715 m. (1) Elizabeth Storr, (2) Sarah Harris, (3) Abigail
4. Sarah m. William House
5. Hannah m. John Waddam
6. Mary m. John Meakins
7. Daniel m. (1) Elizabeth, (2) Dorothy Benton

John Bidwell's inventory was taken June 4, 1687 and totaled 419 pounds, 10 shillings, and 6 pence.
(Families of Early Hartford, Connecticut, p. 57-58)

His name was on the list of "Owners of Land in Hartford before 1653". 
 
 Spouse:
  Sarah Wilcox Bidwell (1623 - 1690) 
 Children:
  Mary Bidwell Meakins (1647 - 1725)
  John Bidwell (1648 - 1692)
  Samuel Bidwell (1650 - 1715)
  Hannah Bidwell Waddams (1655 - 1696)
  Daniel Bidwell (1655 - 1719)
********************
Edwin M. Bidwell, "Genealogy of the First Seven Generations of the Bidwell Family in America" (Albany, N.Y.: Joel Munsell's Sons, 1884), 25-30. Found at the Historical Society of Pennsylvania, 11 November 1993 (JLGH).

John Bidwell married Sarah Wilcox, daughter of John Wilcox. Their children were John, Joseph, Samuel, Sarah, Hannah, Mary, and Daniel. He was one of the early settlers of Hartford. He had a tan yard on an island in Little river in what is now Bushnell's Park [America's oldest publicly funded park, still "a jewel of downtown Hartford" in 2020]. It details his land, a court case, and his will and inventory. 

WILL, 10 February: John names his wife, Sarah, and children John, Joseph, Samuel, Daniel, Sarah House, Hannah Waddams, Mary Meekins. He wills to them a lot in the swamp, lands, buildings, and appurtenances, money in pounds, an upper lot in the swamp, a house lot,  household goods, cattle, chattels, houses, four acres in the meadow pasture beginning at the old ditch. He made a codicil on 22 August 1683. John Bidwell signed by mark.

INVENTORY, 4 June 1687: [Briefly[ wearing apparel, sheets, chests, box, bedstead and cord, 4 chairs, warming pan, napkins, pillow been [case?], table cloths, towels, 2 brass kettle, kettle, 2 skillets, 2 iron pots, tongs, 3 tyred iron, spit, fire iron, mortar & pestle, 5 pewter, wheat, churn, pails, tubs and woodenware, table, ride, kneeding trough, fry pan, cheese pry, 2 barrels, 2 trammels, trowel, cupboard, tin ware, hower glass wine glass bottles, earthenware, books, bedding, wool, wheat, Indian [corn?], 2 oxen, 3 cows, 2 steers 3 years old, 2 mares, 1 horse colt 2 years old, 10 sheep, 4 lambs, 6 swine, cart, yoke collar traces, 1 chain, plow, axes and hews, rope, the homestead, ten acres of upland in fence, dwelling house, barn, outland, wasteland, house lot in the swamp, middle lot about 8 acres, the upper lot in the swamp about 4 acres, the pasture about 7 acres, a gun and some yarn. Value in pounds 419 10 06.

nameMeanings.name: Bidwell
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from Bedwell (Essex, Hertfordshire), Bedlar's Green (Essex), Bidwell (Bedfordshire, Northamptonshire, Devon, Somerset), or Biddles Farm (Buckinghamshire), all of which were named as ‘the spring or stream in a shallow valley’ from Old English bydewelle (composed of the elements byde(n) ‘tub’ + well(a) ‘spring, stream’).

variants.name: Bodwell
meanings: English: perhaps a variant of   Bidwell   orpossibly   Bardwell  .

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: The Pilgrims Board the Mayflower
historicEvents.description: "The Pilgrims boarded the Mayflower in search of religious freedom in the New World in September 1620. The Mayflower set sail from Plymouth, England, with 102 passengers, 40 of whom called themselves ""Saints."" After two stormy months at sea, the ship reached the New World. Nearly half of the group died during their first winter due to malnutrition and the harsh New England winter."

en.lifeSummary: John Bidwell was born about 1615, in England. He married Sarah Wilcox in 1640, in Hartford, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America. They were the parents of at least 4 sons and 3 daughters. He lived in Hartford, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America in 1640. He died from 22 August 1683 to 4 June 1687, in Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America.

ko.lifeSummary: John Bidwell(은)는 대략 1615년 영국에서 태어났다. 그는 1640년 브리티시 콜론이앨 어머릭아 코네티컷 주 하트퍼드 하트퍼드에서 Sarah Wilcox(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 네 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 1640년도에 브리티시 콜론이앨 어머릭아 코네티컷 주 하트퍼드 하트퍼드에서 살았다. 그는 1683년 8월 22일부터 1687년 6월 4일까지 브리티시 콜론이앨 어머릭아 코네티컷 주 하트퍼드에서 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: John Bidwell родился около 1615 г. в местности Англия. Он женился на женщине по имени Sarah Wilcox в 1640 г. в местности хартфорд, хартфорд, Коннектикут, бритиш колоняль амирика. У них было по крайней мере 4 сына и 3 дочери. Он жил в местности хартфорд, хартфорд, Коннектикут, бритиш колоняль амирика в 1640 году. Он умер с 22 августа 1683 по 4 июня 1687 в местности хартфорд, Коннектикут, бритиш колоняль амирика.

es.lifeSummary: John Bidwell nació aproximadamente 1615, en Inglaterra. Se casó con Sarah Wilcox en 1640, en Hartford, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America. Fueron padres de por lo menos 4 hijos y 3 hijas. Vivió en Hartford, Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America en 1640. Murió desde 22 de agosto de 1683 hasta 4 de junio de 1687, en Hartford, Connecticut Colony, British Colonial America.
